---
title: "Inference"
---

This part moves from describing data to drawing formal conclusions from them.
Here the question changes from “what do the data look like?” to “what can I
reasonably claim beyond this sample?” That shift introduces hypotheses, decision
rules, assumptions, and the standard inferential tools used to compare groups or
assess association.

The emphasis is not on memorising named tests. It is on learning how inferential
questions differ, how assumptions shape what a result means, and how to match a
method to the design and data structure that generated the evidence. The chapters
therefore build from general inferential logic into specific families of tests and
then close with a decision guide that shows how these pieces fit together.

Once this logic is established, the book can move beyond isolated tests and into
models that describe responses, predictors, and structure more explicitly.
